  • 1. Acquisition schedule
  • 2. Per-protein abundance heatmap by organism
  • 3. Per-protein effect size per pairwise comparison
  • 4. Per-protein within-condition spread by organism
  • 5. Mean–variance trend by organism

Mix of Proteome benchmark — design visualization

Author

Generated to accompany Mix_of_Proteome_design.md

This notebook visualises the three-organism Mix_of_Proteome benchmark (E. coli + Human + Yeast at known proportions). Read alongside Mix_of_Proteome_design.md. Unlike the CSF / K562 dilution-series benchmarks, this dataset has a built-in null class (Human, 50 % in every condition) and a built-in positive class (E. coli and Yeast, varied) — no swap is needed.

Code 
library(data.table)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(patchwork)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 11))

annotation = fread("Mix_of_Proteome_annotation.csv")
idmap      = fread("idmapping.tsv")

# Load (or recompute) summarized protein-level data
sum_path = "MSstats/msstats_summarized.rda"
if (!file.exists(sum_path)) {
  library(MSstats)
  msstats_input = fread("MSstats/msstats_input.csv") |> as.data.frame()
  msstats_input$AnomalyScores = NA
  msstats_summarized = dataProcess(msstats_input,
                                   normalization  = FALSE,
                                   featureSubset  = "topN",
                                   n_top_feature  = 100,
                                   MBimpute       = TRUE,
                                   summaryMethod  = "TMP",
                                   numberOfCores  = 4)
  save(msstats_summarized, file = sum_path)
} else {
  load(sum_path)
}

prot = as.data.table(msstats_summarized$ProteinLevelData)
prot[, Protein := as.character(Protein)]
prot[, GROUP   := as.character(GROUP)]

# Attach Organism (3-class label: Human / E. coli / Yeast / NA)
org_lookup = idmap[, .(Protein = Entry,
                        Organism = fcase(
                          grepl("Homo sapiens",      Organism), "Human",
                          grepl("Escherichia coli",  Organism), "E. coli",
                          grepl("Saccharomyces|yeast", Organism, ignore.case = TRUE), "Yeast",
                          default = NA_character_))]
prot = merge(prot, org_lookup, by = "Protein", all.x = TRUE)

1. Acquisition schedule

Each row is one MS run. Order is injection order; Condition codes the mixing ratio (E.coli / Human / Yeast in %). Human is held at 50 % in every run; only E. coli (5 → 45 %) and Yeast (45 → 5 %) vary.

Code 
ann_plot = copy(annotation)
ann_plot[, Ecoli := as.integer(str_extract(Condition, "(?<=E)[0-9]+"))]
ann_plot[, Yeast := as.integer(str_extract(Condition, "(?<=Y)[0-9]+"))]

cond_levels = ann_plot[order(Ecoli), unique(Condition)]
ann_plot[, Condition := factor(Condition, levels = cond_levels)]

ggplot(ann_plot, aes(x = Order, y = Condition, fill = Ecoli)) +
  geom_point(shape = 21, size = 6, colour = "white", stroke = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = BioReplicate), size = 2.6, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#fee5d9", high = "#a50f15",
                      name = "E. coli %", limits = c(0, 50)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:18, expand = expansion(add = 0.5)) +
  labs(title = "Run schedule (Order × Condition)",
       subtitle = "Number inside each marker = BioReplicate. Conditions ordered by E. coli %.",
       x = "Injection order", y = NULL) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_line(colour = "grey90"),
        panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_text(family = "mono"),
        legend.position = "right")

The 18 runs are not blocked by condition — they are interleaved in injection order, which spreads any temporal drift across all six conditions.

2. Per-protein abundance heatmap by organism

Rows are proteins (sampled to 250 per organism for legibility), columns are runs sorted by condition (E5 → E45). Each row is z-scored. Top facet = Human (expected flat across columns), middle = E. coli (expected left-to-right gradient), bottom = Yeast (expected right-to-left gradient).

Code 
run_order = ann_plot[order(Condition, Order), .(originalRUN = R.FileName, Condition)]

prot_h = prot[!is.na(Organism) & originalRUN %in% run_order$originalRUN]

# z-score per protein
prot_h[, z := scale(LogIntensities)[, 1], by = Protein]
prot_h[, z := pmax(pmin(z, 2), -2)]

# Sample 250 proteins per organism
set.seed(42)
keep_proteins = prot_h[, .(P = unique(Protein)), by = Organism][
  , .(P = sample(P, min(.N, 250))), by = Organism]
prot_h = prot_h[Protein %in% keep_proteins$P]

# Cluster rows within each organism
make_row_order = function(dat) {
  m   = dcast(dat, Protein ~ originalRUN, value.var = "z")
  pids = m$Protein
  mat = as.matrix(m[, -1, with = FALSE]); mat[is.na(mat)] = 0
  pids[if (nrow(mat) > 1) hclust(dist(mat))$order else 1]
}
ord_by_org = prot_h[, .(P = list(make_row_order(.SD))), by = Organism]
prot_h[, Protein := factor(Protein,
                            levels = unlist(ord_by_org$P))]
prot_h[, originalRUN := factor(originalRUN, levels = run_order$originalRUN)]
prot_h[, Organism := factor(Organism, levels = c("Human", "E. coli", "Yeast"))]

col_strip = run_order[, .(originalRUN = factor(originalRUN, levels = run_order$originalRUN), Condition)]
p_strip = ggplot(col_strip, aes(x = originalRUN, y = 1, fill = Condition)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Spectral", name = "Condition") +
  scale_x_discrete(expand = c(0, 0)) + scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  theme_void() +
  theme(legend.position = "top", plot.margin = margin(0, 0, 0, 0))

p_heat = ggplot(prot_h, aes(x = originalRUN, y = Protein, fill = z)) +
  geom_tile() +
  facet_grid(Organism ~ ., scales = "free_y", space = "free_y", switch = "y") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#2ca02c", mid = "grey90", high = "#d62728",
                       midpoint = 0, limits = c(-2, 2),
                       name = "row z-score") +
  scale_x_discrete(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(title = "Per-protein intensity by organism",
       subtitle = "Human ~ flat. E. coli increases left→right (E5→E45). Yeast decreases left→right.",
       x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
        axis.text.y = element_blank(), axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
        strip.text.y.left = element_text(angle = 0, face = "bold"),
        legend.position = "right",
        panel.spacing.y = unit(0.4, "lines"))

p_strip / p_heat + plot_layout(heights = c(0.04, 1), guides = "collect") &
  theme(legend.position = "right")

What to see:

  • Human (top). No left/right gradient — rows are scrambled red/green noise. This is the null class.
  • E. coli (middle). Clear gradient: green on the left (E5, E10) → red on the right (E40, E45).
  • Yeast (bottom). Mirror of E. coli: red on the left, green on the right.

3. Per-protein effect size per pairwise comparison

For each of the 15 condition pairs, plot the density of mean_log2(c1) − mean_log2(c2) split by organism. Vertical dashed lines mark the expected log2FC for each organism in that contrast.

Code 
# Pairwise contrasts (matches the 15 in Mixture_of_proteomes_processing.R)
cond_pct = unique(ann_plot[, .(Condition,
                                 E = Ecoli, H = 50, Y = Yeast)])
pairs_dt = CJ(c1 = cond_pct$Condition, c2 = cond_pct$Condition)[
  as.character(c1) < as.character(c2)]

pairs_dt = merge(pairs_dt, cond_pct[, .(Condition, E1 = E, Y1 = Y)],
                 by.x = "c1", by.y = "Condition")
pairs_dt = merge(pairs_dt, cond_pct[, .(Condition, E2 = E, Y2 = Y)],
                 by.x = "c2", by.y = "Condition")
pairs_dt[, `:=`(exp_Human = 0,
                 `exp_E. coli` = log2(E1 / E2),
                 exp_Yeast    = log2(Y1 / Y2))]
pairs_dt[, Contrast := paste(c1, "vs", c2)]

per_cond = prot[!is.na(GROUP), .(mean_log = mean(LogIntensities, na.rm = TRUE)),
                 by = .(Protein, GROUP)]
per_cond_w = dcast(per_cond, Protein ~ GROUP, value.var = "mean_log")

eff_long = pairs_dt[, {
  c1n = as.character(c1); c2n = as.character(c2)
  diff_vec = per_cond_w[[c1n]] - per_cond_w[[c2n]]
  data.table(Protein = per_cond_w$Protein, diff = diff_vec)
}, by = .(Contrast, c1, c2,
           exp_Human, `exp_E. coli`, exp_Yeast)]
eff_long = merge(eff_long, org_lookup, by = "Protein")
eff_long = eff_long[!is.na(Organism) & is.finite(diff)]

# Long format for expected lines
exp_long = melt(unique(pairs_dt[, .(Contrast,
                                      Human   = exp_Human,
                                      `E. coli` = `exp_E. coli`,
                                      Yeast   = exp_Yeast)]),
                id.vars = "Contrast",
                variable.name = "Organism", value.name = "expected")

ggplot(eff_long, aes(x = diff, fill = Organism)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, colour = NA) +
  geom_vline(data = exp_long,
             aes(xintercept = expected, colour = Organism),
             linetype = 2, linewidth = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ Contrast, ncol = 3, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Human" = "#7f7f7f",
                                "E. coli" = "#1f77b4",
                                "Yeast" = "#2ca02c")) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Human" = "#7f7f7f",
                                  "E. coli" = "#1f77b4",
                                  "Yeast" = "#2ca02c"),
                      guide = "none") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-4.5, 4.5)) +
  labs(title = "Per-protein log2 fold-change by contrast and organism",
       subtitle = "Dashed lines = expected log2FC from the mixing design.",
       x = "log2(c1) − log2(c2)", y = "density", fill = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        strip.text = element_text(size = 8))

Human density is centred on 0 in every panel (it is the null class). E. coli and Yeast densities are centred on the expected value from the mixing design, with the symmetric inversion between organisms visible across panels (where E. coli is shifted left, Yeast is shifted right).

4. Per-protein within-condition spread by organism

Pooled within-condition SD per protein, by organism. Unlike CSF/K562, no operation has been applied to one organism but not the others — so the distributions should largely overlap.

Code 
per_run_stats = prot[!is.na(GROUP) & !is.na(Organism),
                      .(mean_log = mean(LogIntensities, na.rm = TRUE),
                        sd_log   = sd(LogIntensities,   na.rm = TRUE),
                        n        = .N),
                      by = .(Protein, GROUP, Organism)]

# Pool over conditions: mean of within-condition SDs per protein, weighted by df
pooled = per_run_stats[, .(sd_pool = sqrt(sum((n - 1) * sd_log^2, na.rm = TRUE) /
                                            max(sum(n - 1, na.rm = TRUE), 1)),
                           mean_intensity = mean(mean_log, na.rm = TRUE)),
                        by = .(Protein, Organism)]
pooled[, Organism := factor(Organism, levels = c("Human", "E. coli", "Yeast"))]

ggplot(pooled[is.finite(sd_pool) & sd_pool > 0],
        aes(x = sd_pool, fill = Organism)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, colour = NA) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Human" = "#7f7f7f",
                                "E. coli" = "#1f77b4",
                                "Yeast" = "#2ca02c")) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 1.5)) +
  labs(title = "Within-condition pooled SD per protein, by organism",
       x = "sd(log2 intensity) inside each condition (pooled)",
       y = "density", fill = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

The three densities sit on top of each other — the within-condition spread is governed by the measurement noise floor, not by which organism a peptide came from. This is why empirical-Bayes variance moderation is not penalised on this benchmark (contrast with CSF/K562 §5).

5. Mean–variance trend by organism

Code 
ggplot(pooled[is.finite(sd_pool) & sd_pool > 0],
        aes(x = mean_intensity, y = sd_pool, colour = Organism)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, size = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, method = "loess") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Human" = "#7f7f7f",
                                  "E. coli" = "#1f77b4",
                                  "Yeast" = "#2ca02c")) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1.5)) +
  labs(title = "Mean–variance relationship by organism",
       subtitle = "Trends overlap — no class-asymmetric variance bias.",
       x = "mean log2 intensity", y = "pooled SD", colour = NULL) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

The three loess curves trace approximately the same downward trend (high SD at low intensity from drop-out / LOQ, low SD at high intensity), with no class-wide vertical separation. Empirical-Bayes moderation sees a single, well-defined mean-variance trend and stabilises variance estimates without biasing one organism relative to another.

6. Take-aways

  1. Ground truth is built into the design: Human = null (50 % in every condition), E. coli + Yeast = positives with known per-comparison log2FC. No swap, no manufactured null.
  2. Effect sizes range from 0.17 to 3.17 log2 units across the 15 pairwise comparisons. Adjacent contrasts (e.g. E10 vs E20) are hard; extreme contrasts (E5 vs E45) are easy.
  3. Within-condition variance is approximately equal across organisms — the structural disadvantage that variance moderation suffers on CSF/K562 does not apply here. Differences between methods on this dataset reflect the methods, not the design.
